text.title: Unit Testing (FIRST + AAA) Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules every application of unit-testing enforces. Each rule has a statement, a rationale anchored to industry practice, and a source citation.

rule.statement: Every unit test satisfies FIRST: Fast (<10ms), Isolated (no shared state), Repeatable (same result every run), Self-validating (pass/fail in code), Timely (written with or before the code).
rule.rationale: Slow or order-dependent tests get disabled; non-self-validating tests rot.

rule.statement: Each test has visible Arrange-Act-Assert sections separated by blank lines or comments.
rule.rationale: Reviewers locate the failing phase from the diff alone.

rule.statement: Test names follow `method_scenario_expected` OR `should_when` OR `given_when_then`. Pick one per codebase and never mix.

rationale.source: Osherove, The Art of Unit Testing

rule.rationale: Mixed conventions force readers to mentally translate; one convention compresses.

rule.statement: Assert on observable behavior, never on private state via reflection or `_internal` access.
rule.rationale: Implementation-coupled tests break on refactor; behavior-coupled tests survive.

rule.statement: Target ≥80% line coverage AND ≥70% branch coverage on new code. Mutation coverage is an audit signal, not a CI gate.
rule.rationale: Line-only coverage misses untested branches; mutation is too slow for every PR.

rule.statement: One logical assertion per test. Use bundled-fact helpers (e.g., `assertUserMatches`) only when the bundle is the unit of behavior.
rule.rationale: Multi-assertion tests stop at the first failure and hide downstream issues.
